transcript:
Proverbs chapter 18, verse 17: "He who states his case first seems right, until the other comes and examines him." There's something about that that's just wise. There's a modern-day proverb that says there are two sides to every story, and that's what this ancient proverb says as well.

We got introduced today to the beginning of the Gospel of Matthew. A couple things to note: in chapter one, we have the genealogy of Jesus Christ. Hopefully some of those names are familiar now. There are four women mentioned by name, or at least referenced, in the genealogy of Jesus, which doesn't always happen. And there's something really unique about those four women. We have Mary, obviously, but the first woman mentioned is Tamar. The next is Ruth. And the next is the wife of Uriah, who we know is Bathsheba.

I find it fascinating that Matthew makes it so clear. Here in the genealogy of Jesus Christ, we have Tamar. Remember the story of Judah and Tamar? Judah was Tamar's father-in-law, and he had given two of his sons to Tamar. He was supposed to give that third son to Tamar, but these sons kept dying. So Tamar took things into her own hands and basically posed as a prostitute, and Judah became the father of Perez and Zerah. That's one story of brokenness.

The next is Ruth. Remember her story, where she lived with her mother-in-law Naomi in the land of Moab? She was a Moabite — she wasn't a Jewish person. And yet when Naomi came back to the Promised Land, Ruth said, "Where you go, I will go. Your people will be my people, and your God will be my God." She met Boaz, and they became the great-grandparents of David.

And then you have David and the wife of Uriah, the queen mother of Solomon, who was Bathsheba. We know the story of David and Bathsheba. So we have these three women mentioned here, revealing stories of brokenness. And yet it's in that brokenness that the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are able to do something incredible with the conception and incarnation of Jesus Christ in time and in this place.

It's just amazing. I can hardly get through the genealogy without getting choked up. It happens virtually every time, because you think — this story we've been reading for the last year spans thousands of years, with so many people who were waiting and longing for the fulfillment of this promise. Finally, this is where we get to that place: "Jacob the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, who is called the Christ."

Something important to note about Joseph. When Joseph found that Mary was pregnant, even though they were betrothed, he knew he wasn't the father. Being a righteous man, he decided to divorce her quietly, because if he divorced her publicly, she would be exposed to shame — which is a technical term meaning she would be put to death.

Now, why would Joseph do this? There are essentially three theories. The first is that Joseph thought Mary was unfaithful and didn't want to see her killed. The second, going back to the early Church Fathers, is that Joseph believed Mary — that an angel had told her the child in her womb was the Son of God — but he saw himself as unworthy of being the foster father of the Son of God. Think about it: Nazareth was a small town, fewer than 300 people. Joseph would know Mary's character, that she was a woman of virtue and purity. The third theory is that Joseph believed Mary but simply didn't know what to do. Not that he felt unworthy, but that he was confused. What do you do in that situation? He wanted to follow the law, but he also believed this woman, so he divorced her quietly.

No one knows which theory is correct — maybe we'll find out in heaven. But then the angel of the Lord appears to Joseph: "Do not be afraid to take Mary your wife into your home, for this child is conceived of the Holy Spirit." And Joseph is told to name him Jesus.

In chapter two, we have the story of the wise men and the traditional gifts: gold, frankincense, and myrrh. But there is a deeper meaning here. Gold symbolizes the kingship of Jesus. Frankincense represents that he is a priest and will be the sacrifice. And myrrh was used to anoint the dead, so even at the moment of his birth, Jesus is recognized on three levels: as king, as priest and sacrifice, and as the one who, as Archbishop Fulton Sheen once said, was the only baby born to die.

But Jesus didn't just come to die. He also came to establish the kingdom. How many references in these first four chapters do we have to the Old Testament? Quite a few — from Isaiah, from Jeremiah. We just listened to those prophets over the last couple weeks, and now we hear their fulfillment. Remember, the Assyrians destroyed the ten tribes in the north, the kingdom of Israel. The Babylonians destroyed the kingdom of Judah. So what does Jesus do? The first moment he makes any public declaration, in chapter four, he says: "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand."

Jesus is here to establish a kingdom, and we see this revealed in where he begins his preaching. He goes to the north of the Sea of Galilee, to Capernaum by the sea. Matthew points out this is in the territory of Zebulun and Naphtali, quoting the prophet: "Land of Zebulun, land of Naphtali, toward the sea, across the Jordan, Galilee of the Gentiles — the people who sat in darkness have seen a great light." Why does he go there? Because when the Assyrians came from the north, the first tribes they annihilated were Zebulun and Naphtali. So when Jesus is reconstituting the kingdom, he goes to the place where all the destruction began.

And then he goes along the shore of Galilee, sees Simon called Peter and his brother Andrew, and says, "Come, follow me." He sees James and John — "Come, follow me." He gathers twelve apostles. Those twelve apostles are the beginning of reconstituting the twelve tribes of Israel. The lost tribes are now being brought together under the king who was promised and is fulfilled in Jesus. This is the new kingdom, the kingdom that was lost but is now reestablished in Jesus.

Here is Jesus fulfilling everything God promised in the Old Testament. And he invites you and me into being part of that kingdom. He establishes us through baptism, and we get to live that all of our lives and into eternity. So let us give our hearts to him, because remember what he said: "Repent" — which means turn around, have a change of heart, a change of life, a change of mind, a change of actions. We repent and turn to the Lord, away from our sins and back to him. That takes grace, of course — not just a strong will, but God's help.

discussion.text: He who states his case first seems right, until the other comes and examines him
Jacob the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, who is called the Christ
Being a righteous man, he decided to divorce her quietly
Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand
Land of Zebulun, land of Naphtali, toward the sea, across the Jordan, Galilee of the Gentiles — the people who sat in darkness have seen a great light
sees Simon called Peter and his brother Andrew, and says, "Come, follow me."
